content.text: A post-launch review is a structured evaluation of a feature or integration after it has been running in production long enough to accumulate meaningful data. For Claude integrations, the review examines whether the integration is performing as expected across four dimensions: technical stability, response quality, user impact, and product alignment. Without a consistent template and cadence, post-launch reviews default to ad hoc conversations that surface only the most visible issues and miss the systemic patterns that accumulate over time.

attrs.alt: Post-launch review cadence: T+7 hot review, T+30 standard review, T+90 quarterly review, T+180 strategic review

content.text: The review cadence above gives every integration four structured checkpoints in its first six months. Each checkpoint has a different focus: the T+7 review catches acute stability problems; the T+30 review evaluates quality and user experience; the T+90 review assesses return on investment and product fit; the T+180 review determines whether the integration's direction remains aligned with broader product strategy. Skipping earlier reviews means the T+30 or T+90 review inherits unresolved issues that should have been addressed in the prior checkpoint.
T+7 Hot Review: Stability and Adoption

The T+7 hot review runs one week after launch. Its scope is narrow: does the integration run without degrading the surrounding system, and are users adopting it at the expected rate? At one week there is not yet enough data to evaluate response quality trends, but there is enough data to detect critical failure modes — error rates above baseline, latency regressions, unexpected model refusals, or adoption that is zero when it should be non-zero.

The unexpected_refusal_count field is specific to Claude integrations. In staging, operators verify that their use case is handled correctly under their system prompt; after launch, real user prompts surface edge cases that staging did not cover. A handful of unexpected refusals in the first week is normal and signals that the system prompt needs refinement. A high refusal count signals that the system prompt is misconfigured for the actual user population and requires immediate attention — users who encounter repeated refusals will abandon the feature before the T+30 review.
T+30 Standard Review: Quality and User Impact

The T+30 review is the first review with enough data to evaluate response quality systematically. At thirty days, the feedback collection pipeline has produced a statistically meaningful sample: thumb ratings, regeneration events, free-text comments, and support tickets have accumulated across the real user population rather than the small sample that was available at T+7. The T+30 review template covers four areas: quality metrics, user experience metrics, operational costs, and the prioritised list of improvements generated from feedback analysis.
Analyse negative feedback categories; identify top 3 issues
Review prompts associated with high-regeneration sessions
Audit abandonment events; check if model output is the cause
Profile token usage; reduce context window if over-sending
Identify dominant ticket category; file as P1 backlog item

The regeneration rate is a signal unique to generative AI features. When a user clicks "regenerate" or "try again", they are signalling that the response was not adequate — but they have not abandoned the feature. A 10–15% regeneration rate is typical for generative interfaces; above 20% signals a systematic quality problem. Unlike thumb-down ratings, regeneration requires no explicit action from the user and is therefore a more honest signal about how often responses miss the mark.

The cost_variance method compares actual per-interaction cost against the pre-launch estimate. A variance above 20% is worth investigating: it usually indicates that real user prompts are longer than the test prompts used in the estimate, that the integration is sending more context than necessary, or that users are hitting retry scenarios at a higher rate than expected. Cost variances discovered at T+30 are correctable with prompt engineering; cost variances discovered at T+90 after a quarter of over-spend require a budget conversation.
T+90 Quarterly Review: Return on Investment and Product Fit

The T+90 review evaluates whether the integration is delivering the value that justified building it. At ninety days, the initial adoption curve has flattened, power users have emerged, and the product team has enough longitudinal data to assess retention. The T+90 review asks harder questions than earlier checkpoints: Is the integration being used for the intended purpose? Are users who use the feature more likely to retain? Has the integration surfaced unexpected use cases that suggest a different direction?

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class QuarterlyReviewData:
    integration_name: str

    # Retention
    day30_retention_with_feature: float   # users who used feature and returned in 30 days
    day30_retention_without_feature: float # matched cohort without feature

    # Usage depth
    median_sessions_per_active_user: float
    power_user_threshold_sessions: int     # e.g. 10 sessions in 30 days
    power_user_pct: float                  # % of active users who are power users

    # ROI
    monthly_api_cost: float
    estimated_monthly_value: float         # conversion, time-saved, support-deflected, etc.
    roi_target: float                      # e.g. 3.0 = 3x cost

    def retention_lift(self) -> float:
        base = self.day30_retention_without_feature
        return (self.day30_retention_with_feature - base) / base if base > 0 else 0.0

    def roi(self) -> float:
        return self.estimated_monthly_value / self.monthly_api_cost if self.monthly_api_cost > 0 else 0.0

    def product_fit_assessment(self) -> str:
        lines = []
        lift = self.retention_lift()
        if lift > 0.05:
            lines.append(f"RETENTION: {lift:.0%} lift — integration drives meaningful retention improvement")
        elif lift > 0:
            lines.append(f"RETENTION: {lift:.0%} lift — positive but small; investigate usage depth")
        else:
            lines.append("RETENTION: no lift — feature may not be meeting user needs")

        roi = self.roi()
        if roi >= self.roi_target:
            lines.append(f"ROI: {roi:.1f}x (target {self.roi_target:.1f}x) — integration is cost-justified")
        else:
            lines.append(f"ROI: {roi:.1f}x below {self.roi_target:.1f}x target — review value model")

        if self.power_user_pct < 0.05:
            lines.append("DEPTH: very few power users — consider whether feature is discoverable enough")

        return "
".join(lines)

The retention_lift calculation compares a matched cohort: users who encountered the Claude integration against similar users who did not. The comparison must control for self-selection bias — users who seek out and use the integration may be more engaged to begin with, which would inflate the apparent retention lift. A valid retention analysis uses propensity score matching or a randomised holdout, not a simple active-user versus total-user comparison. If the T+90 review shows no retention lift, the integration may be a nice-to-have rather than a core value driver, which should inform the T+180 strategic review.
T+180 Strategic Review: Direction and Roadmap Alignment

The T+180 review is the first review that answers a strategic question rather than an operational one: should this integration continue in its current form, be expanded, be redesigned, or be retired? At six months, the team has enough evidence to make a confident strategic decision. The review covers the integration's fit with current product strategy, whether the model capabilities available at launch are still the right ones (or whether newer model versions have changed the design calculus), and whether the integration has created technical debt that is beginning to constrain the broader product.
Metrics are healthy, no major issues, product strategy unchanged. Reduce review frequency to quarterly after T+180.
Integration is successful; add adjacent capabilities or extend to new user segments. Treat expansion as a new feature launch with its own review cadence.
Metrics indicate the current approach has a structural limitation (wrong model, wrong prompt architecture, wrong UX). Plan a redesign sprint and relaunch the review cadence from T+7.
Integration is not delivering value and the root cause is not addressable. Begin decommissioning with the data retention and communication plan process.

The redesign decision is the most common outcome at T+180 for early-stage Claude integrations. The model capabilities available when the integration was designed evolve significantly over a six-month period — a feature that was built around a workaround for a model limitation may be able to remove that workaround with a newer model version. Similarly, the prompt engineering patterns that teams learn from six months of production feedback often suggest a fundamentally better prompt architecture than the one that shipped at launch. A redesign at T+180 is not a failure; it is the natural product of the improvement loop working as intended.

Post-launch reviews are not status updates. A status update describes what happened; a review evaluates whether what happened is good enough and decides what to do next. The distinction matters for who should attend and how the meeting should be run. Status updates can be asynchronous; reviews require the people who can make decisions about the integration's direction to be present and engaged.

The requires_escalation method flags decisions that have product-level implications. A decision to retire an integration or initiate a major redesign affects roadmap commitments, user communications, and potentially contractual obligations for enterprise customers. These decisions should be escalated to product leadership before being communicated externally — the review itself makes the recommendation, but the escalation confirms the decision is aligned with the broader product strategy before it becomes an action.
Adjusting Review Timing for Feature Scale

The four-checkpoint cadence above is calibrated for a standard feature launch. For small experimental features (beta access for a subset of users), the cadence can be compressed — T+7 and T+30 reviews may be combined if the user sample is too small for the T+7 data to be statistically meaningful. For large-scale launches (a significant capability shipped to the full user base), the cadence should be expanded — a T+3 emergency review should be added if the launch is associated with a known risk that may materialise within days.
T+7 sample too small; combine with T+14
Default cadence for most feature launches
Extra T+3 checkpoint for high-visibility launches
24-hour check for content policy or safety risk features

The high-risk / safety-sensitive row covers launches where Claude is being used in a new domain or with a new audience that has not been validated in production before. A 24-hour T+1 review is not a full review — it is a targeted check of safety metrics only: refusal rates, safety filter trigger rates, and any user reports flagging harmful or inappropriate content. If the T+1 check is clean, the integration proceeds through the standard cadence; if it surfaces safety concerns, the feature can be paused before it has been exposed to a large user population.

Post-launch reviews give every Claude integration four structured checkpoints in its first six months: a T+7 hot review for stability and adoption, a T+30 standard review for quality and user impact, a T+90 quarterly review for ROI and product fit, and a T+180 strategic review for direction and roadmap alignment. The HotReviewChecklist, StandardReviewSnapshot, and QuarterlyReviewData data structures provide a machine-readable representation of each review's inputs that can be stored, versioned, and queried. Review timing adjusts to launch scale: micro experiments can combine early checkpoints, large rollouts add a T+3 checkpoint, and safety-sensitive launches add a T+1 safety-only check. The four strategic outcomes at T+180 — continue, expand, redesign, retire — connect the improvement review to the decommissioning and evolution processes that follow.
